nonSdsHeading: Non-Student Direct Stream (Non-SDS) / General Student Visa
nonSdsPara1: While the Student Direct Stream (SDS) program offers a streamlined application process for select countries, the Non-SDS Canada Student Visa program extends this opportunity to students from all corners of the globe.
nonSdsPara2: The Non-SDS or General Student Visa is an option for international students who do not meet the eligibility requirements for the Student Direct Stream (SDS) program. The SDS program offers a streamlined visa application process with faster processing times for certain eligible students from India and Brazil. Non-SDS applicants, on the other hand, must undergo a more comprehensive application process, which may take longer to process.
nonSdsPara3: While Non-SDS or General Student Visa offers greater flexibility in terms of nationality and program selection, it also entails a lengthier application process and more stringent documentation requirements. Let's look into the pros and cons of this visa to help you make an informed decision.
ProsLi2: Allows students from all countries to apply
ProsLi3: More flexibility in choosing a DLI
ProsLi4: No language score requirements for certain programs
e1: You need to have a valid offer of admission from a designated learning institution (DLI) in Canada.
e2: Hold a provincial attestation letter (PAL) from the province or territory where you plan to study.
e3: Demonstrate adequate English language proficiency, typically with an IELTS score of 6.0 for undergraduate studies and 6.5 for postgraduate studies. See Chart Below for Other Accepted Language tests and its requirements.
e4: Show sufficient funds to support yourself during your studies in Canada. This may include personal savings, parental support, or external scholarships.
e5: Also, you should meet all other Canadian immigration requirements, such as criminal background checks and medical exams.
eligibTab2Value1: Overall 6.0 - 5.5 acceptable in one of the modules
eligibTab2Value2: Overall 6.5 - no less than 6 in any of the modules
eligibTab2Value7: Score of at least 7 for French*

ap6: Your letter of acceptance from a DLI.
ap9: Police certificates from your home country and any other country you have lived in for more than six months.

ProofFundTableOpenPermitPara: As of March 19, 2024, to be eligible to apply for a Spousal Open Work Permit as an international student, you must be enrolled in one of the following programs:
GradProgPara: Graduate programs encompass master’s and doctorate degrees granted by universities or polytechnic institutions.
ProfDegProgSubHead: Professional degree programs include the following:
ProfDegProgList2: Bachelor of Law or Juris Doctor (LLB, JD, BCL)
ProfDegProgList7: Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BScN, BSN, BNSc)
RefusalHeading: Don’t forget to avoid these common Non-SDS or General Student Visa refusal reasons and increase your chances of approval.
r1: Your application is incomplete, and you did not share all the required documentation.
r2: You do not have enough money to support yourself during your stay in the country.
r3: Your health condition poses a risk to public health.
r4: You submitted fraudulent documents in your application.
r5: You may have violated the terms of your previous visa, such as overstaying or working illegally.
inCase1: If you have faced a refusal for any of the reasons mentioned above, don't worry. With over 10 years of experience, we specialize in handling previously refused cases. We have successfully secured approval for students who have had multiple refusals, long gaps in education, and are of mature age.
inCase2: We achieve this by tailoring our approach to your specific case and addressing each concern raised by the officer in previous refusals. Using case law, we identify similar cases with positive outcomes and apply them as precedents in the cases we handle. This is why we have a high success rate, with over 90% approval in such cases. At Brightlight Immigration, we have a dedicated team of visa application specialists who can assist you from the start of the application process all the way to obtaining your visa. Start your process now.
submitHeading: If you submit your study permit application on or after November 1, 2024
submitSubHead: NOTE: Changes were Announced to the Post Graduate Work Permit (PGWP) Eligibility - September 18, 2024
submitSubPara: If you submit your study permit application on or after November 1, 2024, you must also meet the new requirements that apply to your situation to be eligible to apply for a PGWP.
submit1List1: If you Graduated from a University Bachelor’s degree, Master’s Degree or Doctoral Degree Program:
sub1Li1SubLi1: Language requirement: You must prove your English or French language skills with a minimum level of Canadian Language Benchmarks (CLB) 7 in English or Niveaux de competence linguistique canadiens (NCLC) 7 in French in all 4 language areas.
sub1Li1SubLi2: Field of study requirement: All fields of study are eligible. There is no additional field of study requirement.
submit2List1: If you Graduated from Any Other University Program:
sub2Li1SubLi1: Language requirement: You must prove your English or French language skills with a minimum level of CLB 7 in English or NCLC 7 in French in all 4 language areas.
sub2Li1SubLi2: Field of study requirement: All fields of study are eligible. There is no additional field of study requirement.
submit3List1: If you graduated from a College Program or Any Other Program Not listed Above:
sub3Li1SubLi1: Language requirement: You must prove your English or French language skills with a minimum level of CLB 5 in English or NCLC 5 in French in all 4 language areas.
sub3Li1SubLi2: Field of study requirement: All fields of study are eligible. There is no additional field of study requirement.

q1: Can I apply for my Non-SDS or General Student Visa online?
qa1: No, you cannot apply for your Non-SDS or General Student Visa online. You must submit your application in person at a Canadian visa application center. Or, we can help you by submitting your application for you for a favorable response.
q2: How long does it take to process a Non-SDS or General Student Visa application?
qa2: The processing time for a Non-SDS Canada Student Visa can vary depending on the complexity of your application and the current workload of the Canadian government processing center. However, it is generally expected to take longer than the processing time for SDS applications, which is typically around 20 calendar days.
q3: Are there any exemptions from the Non-SDS or General Student Visa requirements?
qa3: Yes, there are a few exemptions from the Non-SDS or General Student Visa requirements. For example, you may be exempt from the language proficiency requirement if you are studying a program in French or English. You may also be exempt from the financial requirement if you are receiving financial assistance from a Canadian government program.
q4: Where can I get proof of funds for my Non-SDS or General Student Visa application?
qa4: You can get proof of funds from a variety of sources, such as personal savings, bank statements, or financial aid documents. You may also be able to use a letter of financial support from your parents or guardians.
q5: What is the language proficiency requirement for the Non-SDS or General Student Visa?
qa5: The language proficiency requirement for the Non-SDS or General Student Visa is typically an IELTS score of 6.0 for undergraduate studies and 6.5 for postgraduate studies. However, there are some exceptions to this rule. For example, you may be exempt from the language proficiency requirement if you are studying a program in French or English.
q6: How can I appeal a decision on my Non-SDS or General Student Visa application?
qa6: If your Non-SDS Visa application is denied, you will have 30 days from the date of the denial to appeal the decision. You can appeal the decision by submitting a letter to the Canadian government explaining why you believe the decision should be overturned. Or we can help you by submitting your reconsideration.
q7: Which is better: SDS or non-SDS?
qa7: SDS applicants typically have a higher approval rate (70%) compared to non-SDS applicants (40%). Additionally, SDS applications are processed much faster.
q8: How many hours can I work while studying in Canada?
qa8: Generally, international students can work up to 20 hours per week while studying. However, a temporary waiver allows students to work more than 20 hours per
q9: Can I change my college once I enter Canada?
qa9: Yes, you can change your college once you have arrived in Canada. However, it is crucial to update Immigration, Refugees, and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) with your new DLI (Designated Learning Institution).
q10: I am failing my courses. What should I do?
qa10: Life happens, and there are various reasons why international students may struggle with their studies. However, maintaining your student status requires meeting the conditions of your study permit. We understand that choosing the wrong course can be a factor in academic struggles. We can help you find alternative courses at different colleges that might be a better fit for your academic strengths and interests. We are connected with over 125 colleges and universities, so we can assist you in finding a suitable program to help you succeed. Please contact us at 604-503-3734 if you are facing academic challenges.
q11: I have been charged with plagiarism. What should I do?
qa11: Plagiarism is a serious offense, and it may result in suspension from your college. If you have been accused of plagiarism, please contact us immediately. We can help you understand the situation and explore options for resolving it. We can also assist you in finding alternative academic pathways that align with your career aspirations.
q12: I have a low GPA and have been suspended from my college. What should I do?
qa12: As an international student, academic struggles can impact your visa status. If you have been suspended due to a low GPA, we can assist you in exploring alternative educational options that fit your academic profile and career goals. We can help you find suitable courses at different colleges that align with your strengths and interests. Please contact us at 604-503-3734 for assistance.
q13: I am an international student. Is my spouse, common law partner eligible for an Open Work Permit?
qa13: As of March 19, 2024, to be eligible to apply for a Spousal Open Work Permit as an international student, you must be enrolled in one of the following programs:
qa14: Graduate programs encompass master’s and doctorate degrees granted by universities or polytechnic institutions.
qa15:
Professional degree programs include the following:
  • Doctor of Dental Surgery (DDS, DMD)
  • Bachelor of Law or Juris Doctor (LLB, JD, BCL)
  • Doctor of Medicine (MD)
  • Doctor of Optometry (OD)
  • Pharmacy (PharmD, BS, BSc, BPharm)
  • Doctor of Veterinary Medicine (DVM)
  • Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BScN, BSN, BNSc)
  • Bachelor of Education (BEd)
  • Bachelor of Engineering (BEng, BE, BASc) (only)
